( ③ ) Drivers who have been awake for 20 hours perform about as poorly as those with a blood-alcohol level of 0.08 percent. ( ④ ) Consequently, drowsy driving is responsible for thousands of accidents each year. ( ⑤ ) Getting consistent, high-quality rest is therefore one of the simplest ways to protect both physical and mental health.

Vocabulary

  • impair 손상시키다
  • chronic 만성적인
  • drowsy 졸린
2 Question

주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 위치는? Even a single night of poor sleep can impair memory and judgment the following day.

Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 3]

Coral reefs owe their brilliant colors to symbiotic algae that live inside coral tissue. ( ① ) When ocean temperatures rise, corals become stressed and expel these algae. ( ② ) Without their colorful partners, the corals turn ghostly white, a phenomenon known as bleaching. ( ③ ) Bleached corals are weaker and more susceptible to disease. ( ④ ) Large-scale bleaching events have become more frequent over the past three decades, threatening reef ecosystems worldwide. ( ⑤ ) Scientists warn that limiting global warming is essential to preserve these biodiverse habitats.

Vocabulary

  • symbiotic 공생의
  • expel 내쫓다
  • susceptible 취약한
3 Question

주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 위치는? If temperatures remain elevated, the algae may never return, causing the coral to die.

Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편

Battery technology has improved rapidly in recent years. ( ① ) Modern lithium-ion packs store more energy while weighing less than their predecessors. ( ② ) As ranges climb above 500 kilometers per charge, so-called range anxiety begins to fade. ( ③ ) Governments are also expanding charging-station networks along major highways. ( ④ ) In addition, many countries now offer tax credits or rebates to purchasers of electric vehicles. ( ⑤ ) Analysts predict that electric cars could account for half of all new car sales by 2030.

Vocabulary

  • predecessor 이전 모델, 선임자
  • range anxiety 주행거리 불안
  • rebate 환급

주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 위치는? As a result, more drivers feel confident making the switch to electric models.

In the late nineteenth century, a group of young painters broke away from the rigid rules of France's art academies. ( ① ) They began painting outdoors to capture changing light and atmosphere. ( ② ) Critics at the time dismissed their loose brushstrokes and bright colors as unfinished and childish. ( ③ ) Nevertheless, the public slowly warmed to their fresh perspective. ( ④ ) Today works by Monet, Renoir, and Degas are celebrated worldwide. ( ⑤ ) Their movement forever altered the course of modern art.

[지문 6]

Plastic trash does not disappear once it enters the ocean. ( ① ) Waves and sunlight gradually break larger items into tiny fragments called microplastics. ( ② ) These fragments can travel vast distances, turning up on remote Arctic beaches and deep-sea trenches alike. ( ③ ) Studies have detected microplastics in seafood, table salt, and even drinking water. ( ④ ) Because plastics attract toxic chemicals, they may concentrate pollutants as they move through the food chain. ( ⑤ ) Reducing single-use plastics on land is therefore critical for protecting marine life.

Vocabulary

  • ingest 섭취하다
  • fragment 파편, 조각
  • pollutant 오염물질
6 Question

주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 위치는? These particles are small enough to be ingested by plankton at the very base of the oceanic food web.

[지문 7]

Centuries ago, trade in China grew so brisk that bulky metal coins became impractical. ( ① ) Merchants began leaving their heavy currency with trusted agents and receiving paper receipts instead. ( ② ) Over time, these receipts started circulating hand-to-hand as payment for goods. ( ③ ) The government soon recognized the convenience of this system and issued officially sanctioned notes. ( ④ ) Counterfeiting remained a persistent challenge, prompting the use of special dyes and intricate designs. ( ⑤ ) The innovation later spread along the Silk Road, influencing monetary systems across Eurasia.

Vocabulary

  • brisk 활발한
  • impractical 비실용적인
  • sanctioned 승인된
7 Question

주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 위치는? This practice laid the groundwork for what we now call paper money.

Sharing personal information online has never been easier—or riskier. ( ① ) Social-media platforms generate revenue by collecting user data and selling targeted advertisements. ( ② ) Meanwhile, data breaches at major corporations have exposed millions of customers' records. ( ③ ) As public concern mounts, several governments have enacted strict privacy laws requiring companies to obtain explicit consent before gathering data. ( ④ ) Critics argue that enforcement remains uneven and penalties too lenient. ( ⑤ ) Ultimately, users must stay vigilant and adjust privacy settings to safeguard their digital identities.

  • breach (데이터) 유출
  • enact 제정하다
  • lenient 관대한

Wildfires play a natural role in many forest ecosystems. ( ① ) Low-intensity burns clear away dead vegetation, making room for new growth. ( ② ) Decades of aggressive fire suppression, however, have allowed flammable material to accumulate. ( ③ ) As a result, today's fires often burn hotter and spread faster than historical ones. ( ④ ) Fire ecologists now advocate selective thinning and controlled burns to restore healthier conditions. ( ⑤ ) Communities located near forests must adapt to increased smoke and the potential for sudden evacuations.

Vocabulary

  • suppression 진압
  • flammable 가연성의
  • advocate 옹호하다

( ④ ) Psychologists suggest that admitting a loss feels like accepting failure, triggering defensive behavior. ( ⑤ ) Recognizing this bias is the first step toward making objectively better choices.

( ② ) Engineers test 3-D-printed joints that cut costs by two-thirds, but regulatory approvals lag behind innovation. ( ③ ) Charity trials demonstrate life-changing mobility, though battery weight still strains shoulders after extended use. ( ④ ) Insurers remain cautious without long-term durability data. ( ⑤ )

( ② ) Survey telescopes monitor millions of stars toward the galactic bulge. ( ③ ) Timing the brightening curve reveals the mass of the unseen companion. ( ④ ) Upcoming missions like Roman Space Telescope will expand the catalog. ( ⑤ ) Microlensing is also sensitive to rogue planets unbound to any star.

Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 1]

(A) Until recently, fossil-fuel plants could ramp production up or down within minutes, thereby smoothing every fluctuation. Such responsiveness kept blackouts rare even on peak-demand days.

(B) As a result, grid managers now pair multi-hour batteries with AI forecasting tools, both of which restore the lost flexibility and cut emissions. These combined systems can discharge within seconds when clouds roll in.

(C) However, the intermittent output of wind and solar breaks that rhythm, and these abrupt gaps leave operators scrambling for backup power. In these cases, costly gas peaker plants must spin up at short notice.

1 Question

주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.

  1. - (C) - (B)
  2. - (A) - (C)
  3. - (C) - (A)
  4. - (A) - (B)
  5. - (B) - (A)
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 2]

(A) However, these very powers fuel anxieties about designer babies and off-target mutations that may ripple through future generations. Public trust erodes whenever a rogue lab flaunts existing guidelines.

(B) CRISPR now lets scientists cut and paste DNA with a precision that once seemed impossible. Such affordability has slashed experiment costs by two orders of magnitude.

(C) Consequently, global bioethics panels are drafting stringent oversight rules and advocating a temporary moratorium on germ-line edits. In these cases, transparency and international consensus are deemed essential.

2 Question

주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.

  1. - (C) - (B)
  2. - (A) - (C)
  3. - (C) - (A)
  4. - (A) - (B)
  5. - (B) - (A)
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 3]

(A) As a result, many operators sign community-solar deals that slash energy bills by up to 40 percent. In these cases, savings are passed on as cheaper greens for local shoppers.

(B) Hydroponic towers now stack lettuce and herbs inside abandoned factories, and these installations harvest year-round regardless of weather. Such vertical designs maximise yield per square metre.

(C) However, the immense electricity needed for LED grow lights inflates operating costs and keeps profit margins razor-thin. These expenses often force small start-ups to shut down within a year.

3 Question

주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.

(C) However, once stable million-qubit machines arrive, traditional public-key systems may collapse overnight, exposing banking and government secrets. In these scenarios, data breaches could snowball globally.

(A) Early adopters cite nutty flavor, but surveys reveal queasiness tied to cultural perception rather than taste.

(B) Scaling edible-insect markets will hinge on marketing that shifts dietary norms beyond eco-jargon.

(C) Celebrity chefs experiment with ant-dust garnishes on haute cuisine, slowly shifting luxury norms.

99 Question

주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.

  1. - (B) - (C)
  2. - (A) - (C)
  3. - (A) - (B)
  4. - (C) - (B)
  5. - (C) - (A)
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 100]

(A) Maintenance crews schedule dives during calm tides, but storms can demolish moorings overnight.

(B) Commercial viability depends on devices that withstand marine forces under relentless surf.

(C) Engineers trial composite polymers and self-healing coatings inspired by squid beaks.

100 Question

주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.

  1. - (B) - (C)
  2. - (A) - (C)
  3. - (A) - (B)
  4. - (B) - (A)
  5. - (C) - (B)
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 1]

On many social-media platforms, algorithms curate content based on the traces you leave: every like, share, and pause becomes data. At first this feels designed just for you, a friendly concierge who knows your taste. Yet the selections grow narrower with each interaction, shutting out unfamiliar viewpoints. Over days and months you forget how much of the world lies outside that bright rectangle. Researchers call this the “echo-chamber” effect, because your own opinions bounce back at you, louder every time. The same sentence from a stranger appears so often that it starts to feel like statistical truth. In reality the sample is hopelessly biased: all you are hearing is yourself. Each digital echo returns, ________ the belief that produced it.

Vocabulary

  • curate 큐레이션하다, 엄선하다
  • concierge 콘시어지, 안내인
  • biased 편향된
1 Question
  1. diluting
  2. reinforcing
  3. disregarding
  4. postponing
  5. regulating
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 2]

Many professionals equate a swollen calendar with productivity, but the two are not identical. People proudly recount how many meetings they attended, e-mails they answered, and notifications they dismissed before breakfast. Strangely, the most mission-critical task often remains untouched at the end of such frantic days. Psychologists label the phenomenon “structured procrastination”: the mind seeks busy work to avoid the truly important. Because the small duties provide an immediate sense of accomplishment, they trick the brain into believing progress has been made. Meanwhile, the high-impact project gathers dust, its deadline inching closer in silence. The busier you feel, the easier it becomes to justify postponement. Paradoxically, activity can camouflage ________.

Vocabulary

  • equate 동일시하다
  • frantic 정신없이 서두는
  • camouflage 위장하다
2 Question
  1. indifference
  2. avoidance
  3. expertise
  4. resilience
  5. curiosity
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 3]

Shoppers often reach for a green-tinted bag bearing the word “biodegradable” and feel virtuous. The term suggests that the plastic will harmlessly melt back into nature, but chemistry is less forgiving. Many so-called biodegradable plastics only fragment into smaller pieces when exposed to sunlight. These micro-particles drift into rivers, then oceans, where they are ingested by plankton and fish. The fragments travel up the food chain, eventually landing on our dinner plates. Every grocery purchase, therefore, becomes part of a planetary circulation system we rarely notice. Environmental scientists warn that the reassuring label may actually increase consumption. The promise of effortless disposal can create ________, dulling the motivation to reduce single-use plastics in the first place.

Vocabulary

  • virtuous 덕이 높은, 고결한
  • fragment 파편이 되다
  • complacency 현실 안주, 자기만족
3 Question
  1. urgency
  2. complacency
  3. scarcity
  4. synergy
  5. transparency
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 4]

College students preparing for exams often reread a chapter the night before the test, convinced that repetition equals mastery. The lines do appear familiar, and familiarity feels safe. Cognitive studies, however, show that massed rereading produces shallow traces in long-term memory. Spacing study sessions several days apart leads to stronger neural connections. When time intervenes, the brain must reconstruct the concept, an effortful process that deepens encoding. The illusion created by immediate repetition seduces learners into closing the book too early. They misinterpret smooth reading for retention. Ironically, the comfort of familiarity can be a predictor of ________ on the actual exam.

Vocabulary

  • massed 집중된
  • intervene 개입하다
  • retention 보유, 기억
4 Question
  1. uncertainty
  2. proficiency
  3. failure
  4. curiosity
  5. improvement
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 5]

Wind turbines spinning over a sunlit ridge symbolize a fossil-free future. Yet even the windiest valley can experience calm days, and solar panels nap beneath thick clouds. Energy analysts caution that renewables must be paired with storage or backup sources. Otherwise, the grid could falter precisely when hospitals and data centers need power most. Batteries, pumped-hydro reservoirs, and smart demand management each solve part of the puzzle. None is trivial to fund or deploy at scale, but postponing them risks outages that erode public trust. Celebrating the rapid growth of green generation is justified, but forgetting the need for resilience would leave the transition ________.

Vocabulary

  • falter 흔들리다, 불안정해지다
  • trivial 사소한
  • resilience 회복력
5 Question
  1. incomplete
  2. economical
  3. accelerated
  4. guaranteed
  5. obsolete
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 6]

In a lecture hall, it is easy to spot the student scrolling through social media; in virtual classrooms, distraction hides behind every browser tab. Studies tracking keyboard activity reveal that learners switch windows every forty seconds on average. Each switch carries a cognitive cost: attention wanders, and working memory must reload the topic when it returns. Participants report feeling efficient, yet post-test scores tell another story. Multitasking particularly hampers the transfer of complex concepts that require sustained focus. Ironically, the freedom afforded by online platforms can undermine the very flexibility they promise. Without deliberate self-regulation, digital learning can become a sophisticated form of ________.

Vocabulary

  • hamper 방해하다
  • self-regulation 자기 조절
  • escapism 현실 도피
6 Question
  1. exploration
  2. escapism
  3. collaboration
  4. mentorship
  5. automation
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 7]

Ask a painter to create anything and she may stare at a blank canvas for days; give her only three colors and a single brush and new ideas often bloom. Innovation researchers have observed this paradox across domains from product design to jazz improvisation. Constraints eliminate infinite possibilities, allowing the mind to dive deeper into a narrower pool. The process is akin to sculpting: removing marble reveals form. When boundaries are absent, choices multiply until decision fatigue sets in. Conversely, a rule as simple as “no digital instruments” can spark novel soundscapes. In this sense, limitation is not the enemy of imagination but a catalyst. A small cage can provide the necessary friction to ________ creativity.

Vocabulary

  • constraint 제약
  • akin to ~와 유사한
  • catalyst 촉매제
7 Question
  1. stifle
  2. ignite
  3. conceal
  4. imitate
  5. postpone
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 8]

After a restless night, minor annoyances—the barking dog, the slow elevator—feel like personal insults. Neuroscientists trace this irritability to the amygdala, the brain’s alarm center, which becomes more reactive when sleep-deprived. Meanwhile, the prefrontal cortex, responsible for emotional regulation, reduces its inhibitory control. The result is a hair-trigger temperament that misreads neutral faces as hostile. Chronic sleep debt compounds the problem, elevating baseline stress hormones and weakening immune defenses. Yet many adults brag about functioning on five hours, equating fatigue with dedication. They do not realize that their frayed patience taxes colleagues and family alike. A single full night of rest can restore balance, underscoring that sleep is a prerequisite, not a luxury, for ________ social interaction.

Vocabulary

  • irritability 과민함, 짜증
  • inhibitory 억제성의
  • frayed 해어진, 신경이 곤두선
8 Question
  1. volatile
  2. equitable
  3. harmonious
  4. superficial
  5. competitive
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 9]

City planners often justify planting street trees by citing reduced air pollution and lower summer temperatures. These benefits are measurable, but residents may notice an even subtler change: a lift in mood during daily commutes. Surveys show that exposure to greenery correlates with lower levels of anxiety and rumination. MRI scans support the self-reports, revealing decreased activity in brain regions linked to depressive thought loops after a walk in a leafy neighborhood. Scientists suspect that the fractal patterns of leaves engage the eye in gentle, restorative ways. From a policy standpoint, a tree costs less than a counseling session. Investing in urban canopy is therefore both environmentally and psychologically prudent. Failing to consider mental health outcomes would mean undervaluing trees’ most human ________.

Vocabulary

  • rumination 반추(되새김 생각)
  • restorative 회복시키는
  • dividend 배당금, 이익
9 Question
  1. liability
  2. obstacle
  3. dividend
  4. alternative
  5. metaphor
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 10]

In scientific publishing, positive results travel faster than negative ones. Journals prefer stories where the experimental drug works, the theory predicts perfectly, or the survey unveils a dramatic trend. Studies that find no effect languish in file drawers, unseen by peers. This imbalance can mislead future researchers who base their hypotheses on what appears to be a consistent success record. When ten labs test a therapy and only the two successful trials are printed, the literature quietly exaggerates efficacy. Policymakers and investors then allocate resources on incomplete evidence. Critics argue that until null findings become publishable, the edifice of knowledge will rest on selectively reported bricks. The prestige economy of academia thus unintentionally nurtures ________.

Vocabulary

  • languish 시들해지다
  • edifice (지식 등의) 체계, 건물
  • distortion 왜곡
10 Question
  1. rigor
  2. confidence
  3. distortion
  4. consensus
  5. serendipity
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 11]

During the pandemic, many employees discovered that video meetings demand a different kind of attention than face-to-face discussions. Participants must stare directly at a lens to appear engaged, yet also monitor a grid of tiny rectangles for subtle facial cues. Psychologists dub this posture “continuous partial gaze,” a cognitively expensive stance seldom required in physical rooms. Over hours, the unbroken eye contact leads to muscle strain and heightened self-consciousness. At the same time, the ever-present mute button forces speakers to over-enunciate, boosting vocal fatigue. Unlike an office table, a screen offers no peripheral vision for quick mental breaks; every glance to a second monitor feels like a public betrayal. Ironically, the technology meant to replicate presence can amplify social pressure. Consequently, multiple studies link prolonged video conferencing with cognitive ________.

Vocabulary

  • dub 별명을 붙이다
  • over-enunciate 과장해서 발음하다
  • overload 과부하
11 Question
  1. alertness
  2. overload
  3. resilience
  4. eagerness
  5. nostalgia
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 12]

Early investors in cryptocurrency often describe the thrill of watching prices double overnight. Market apps flash celebratory fireworks, and online forums overflow with tales of spontaneous wealth. Yet each upward spike lures more speculators whose fear of missing out inflates demand further. Economists warn that feedback loops of emotion rather than fundamentals drive many of these rallies. When values plunge, newcomers exit en masse, leaving late buyers with steep losses. The pattern repeats with new coins because the memory of risk fades faster than the lure of easy gain. The cheerful digital confetti can therefore create an illusion of financial ________ that the underlying volatility does not support.

Vocabulary

  • spontaneous 자발적인, 즉흥적인
  • en masse 떼를 지어, 집단으로
  • volatility 변동성
12 Question
  1. security
  2. liquidity
  3. anonymity
  4. taxation
  5. philanthropy
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 13]

Seawater absorbs roughly a quarter of the carbon dioxide humans release each year. This uptake slows atmospheric warming, but it alters ocean chemistry, lowering pH in a process called acidification. Coral skeletons built from calcium carbonate dissolve more easily under acidic conditions, weakening entire reef structures. Fish larvae that rely on the reefs for shelter struggle to survive, disrupting food webs that support millions of people. The chain reaction illustrates how one invisible chemical shift can cascade through visible ecosystems. Marine biologists now monitor shell thickness in mollusks as a proxy for overall health. Ignoring the subtle chemical signals would mean overlooking a threat destined to ________ the decline of coastal biodiversity.

Vocabulary

  • uptake 흡수
  • mollusk 연체동물
  • proxy 대리, 지표
13 Question
  1. reverse
  2. accelerate
  3. disguise
  4. localize
  5. trivialize
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 14]

Community organizers often recruit volunteers by emphasizing altruism, yet surveys show personal benefit is an equally powerful motivator. People who lend their skills at food banks or tutoring programs report heightened life satisfaction weeks later. Neurologists attribute the glow to increased activity in reward circuits similar to those triggered by monetary bonuses. Even small acts, such as delivering groceries to an elderly neighbor, can spark the effect. Crucially, the boost persists longer than the pleasure of buying material goods. This suggests that time, not money, may be the more efficient currency of happiness. Paradoxically, serving others becomes a reliable pathway to one’s own emotional ________.

Vocabulary

  • altruism 이타주의
  • attribute to ~의 덕분으로 돌리다
  • well-being 행복, 안녕
14 Question
  1. exhaustion
  2. turmoil
  3. well-being
  4. detachment
  5. indifference

[지문 15]

News consumers claim they want impartial coverage, but click-through rates tell another story. Headlines framed to confirm existing beliefs consistently outperform neutral summaries. Editors face pressure from metrics dashboards that update in real time, rewarding sensationalism with advertising revenue. Over months, algorithmic feedback nudges entire outlets toward ideological edges. The cycle erodes public trust because each side perceives the other as increasingly extreme. Media ethicists warn that without structural changes, the commercial model will continue to bias story selection. In this environment, genuine journalistic ________ becomes the costliest commodity of all.

Vocabulary

  • impartial 공정한
  • sensationalism 선정주의
  • neutrality 중립성
15 Question
  1. brevity
  2. neutrality
  3. humor
  4. redundancy
  5. suspense
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 16]

Wearable fitness trackers promise to nudge users toward healthier lifestyles by measuring every step and heartbeat. Initial data spikes often motivate bursts of enthusiasm: people park farther away, take lunchtime walks, and compete with friends for badge icons. Within months, however, activity levels frequently return to baseline. Behavioral economists suggest that external rewards crowd out intrinsic motivation; users exercise for points rather than pleasure. Once the novelty wanes, the device feels more like a watchdog than a coach, and daily goals become negotiable. Ironically, precise measurement can relieve people of personal responsibility by outsourcing judgment to an algorithm. Without complementary habit-building strategies, quantified health may fail to improve long-term ________.

Vocabulary

  • nudge (특정 방향으로) 유도하다
  • intrinsic 내재적인
  • compliance 준수, 따름
16 Question
  1. compliance
  2. metabolism
  3. flexibility
  4. inheritance
  5. diagnosis
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 17]

Ancient orators memorized hours-long speeches by imagining grand buildings filled with vivid objects—a flaming sword on the staircase, a singing bird beside a column. Modern cognitive scientists confirm that spatial storytelling leverages the brain’s natural talent for navigation. Each object serves as a mental hook from which facts can hang, forming durable associative chains. Students who replace rote rehearsal with such imagery recall material weeks later with minimal review. The method, known as the memory palace, scales from grocery lists to legal arguments. Practice is required, but the investment yields an almost unfair academic advantage. By converting abstract data into concrete scenes, learners strengthen ________ between disparate ideas.

Vocabulary

  • orator 연설가
  • leverage 활용하다
  • disparate 이질적인
17 Question
  1. conflicts
  2. associations
  3. hierarchies
  4. distractions
  5. authorities
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 18]

Popular self-help books praise the power of micro-habits—tiny actions so easy they evade resistance. Floss just one tooth, write one sentence, or perform two push-ups, the authors advise. The logic is that starting small lowers the activation energy for behavior change. Over time, the brain begins to expect the sequence and extends it without conscious effort. Critics argue that microscopic steps lack ambition, but data reveal surprising persistence rates. Once initiated, a behavior chain often grows until it resembles the very goal it once tiptoed toward. Thus, microscopic routines can generate disproportionate ________ that compound into large-scale transformation.

Vocabulary

  • evade 피하다
  • disproportionate 불균형적으로 큰
  • momentum 가속도, 탄력
18 Question
  1. momentum
  2. distraction
  3. entropy
  4. complacency
  5. backlash

[지문 19]

Ride-sharing apps advertise flexible schedules and entrepreneurial freedom, yet drivers’ earnings fluctuate daily with surge pricing. A single traffic jam or vehicle repair can wipe out a week’s profit margin, transferring risk from company to contractor. Because algorithms adjust fares in opaque ways, predicting income becomes a statistical gamble. Sociologists note that financial instability bleeds into health outcomes when workers postpone medical check-ups to keep cars on the road. Conventional safety nets like paid leave seldom apply to independent contractors. Policymakers debate portable benefits funded through platform fees, but implementation lags behind industry growth. Until structural protections emerge, gig-economy labor will remain synonymous with economic ________.

Vocabulary

  • fluctuate 변동하다
  • opaque 불투명한
  • synonymous with ~와 동의어인

  2. philanthropy
  3. uncertainty
  4. monopoly
  5. abundance

[지문 20]

Streaming services boast catalogs that dwarf any physical video store in history. Paradoxically, viewers often spend longer browsing thumbnails than watching shows. Recommendation algorithms prioritize popularity, gradually steering attention toward a handful of blockbuster titles. Independent films buried deep in the menu receive fewer clicks, feeding data back into the popularity loop. Cultural critics worry that a near-infinite shelf still converges on a narrow slice of content. Without editorial interventions, the long tail of creativity may wither from neglect. Digital abundance does not guarantee artistic ________ if discovery tools reward only what is already trending.

Vocabulary

  • dwarf 왜소하게 만들다
  • converge on ~으로 수렴하다
  • visibility 가시성, 눈에 잘 띔
20 Question
  1. visibility
  2. conformity
  3. chronology
  4. censorship
  5. solemnity

[지문 21]

Debates over AI-generated artwork often focus on the novelty of machine creativity, yet a quieter question lingers in legal circles: Who owns the resulting images? Training datasets scrape millions of photos without explicit permission, blending styles in ways no human could track. Artists complain that their signature brushstrokes reappear in uncanny composites. Meanwhile, tech companies argue that the output is transformative and therefore free of infringement. Courts struggle because copyright law was drafted for human authorship, not probabilistic diffusion models. Until precedent emerges, platforms monetize the gray area by selling subscription credits. The uncertainty encourages rapid experimentation but discourages serious investment from traditional galleries. The lack of clear guidelines risks creating a cultural commons where value is extracted without ________ to the original creators.

Vocabulary

  • uncanny 기묘한
  • infringement 침해
  • tribute 헌사, 공물
21 Question
  1. tribute
  2. access
  3. relevance
  4. hostility
  5. nostalgia

[지문 22]

Memory feels like a recording, yet psychologists demonstrate that each recollection rewrites the past. When you tell a friend about last year’s vacation, sensory fragments fuse with present emotions, updating the neural file. Over dozens of retellings, your anecdote drifts subtly from reality, favoring coherent narrative over messy truth. Eyewitness testimony therefore grows less reliable with rehearsal, not more. Courtrooms that treat confident recall as gold standard misunderstand the pliability of the brain. Researchers advise asking witnesses for free narratives before any discussion introduces bias. Ironically, the very act of securing evidence may corrupt it. Justice systems must adapt or risk convictions built on memories that feel vivid yet remain ________.

Vocabulary

  • recollection 회상
  • pliability 유연성
  • malleable 가변적인, 영향을 잘 받는
22 Question
  1. malleable
  2. indelible
  3. symmetrical
  4. peripheral
  5. tangible

[지문 23]

Fast-fashion retailers release new collections weekly, seducing consumers with rock-bottom prices. Garments stitched from cheap synthetics tear after a handful of washes, driving shoppers back for replacements. Landfills now receive truckloads of discarded clothing every minute, and microfibers shed during laundry contaminate waterways. Environmental campaigns urge buyers to choose quality over quantity, yet social-media influencers flaunt hauls that reward volume. Governments debate extended producer responsibility fees, hoping to shift disposal costs upstream. Without economic signals, the cycle of buy-wear-dump will persist. Critics warn that celebrating affordability without factoring externalities amounts to subsidizing ________ waste.

Vocabulary

  • seduce 유혹하다
  • externality 외부 효과
  • disposable 일회용의
23 Question
  1. durable
  2. disposable
  3. ceremonial
  4. strategic
  5. medicinal
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 24]

Urban heat islands make summertime temperatures in dense neighborhoods several degrees higher than in nearby suburbs. Asphalt absorbs sunlight by day and releases it slowly after sunset, trapping residents in an invisible oven. Elderly people without air conditioning face elevated risks of heatstroke. City officials plant reflective roofs and pocket parks, but results lag behind rising emissions. Energy demand spikes as millions crank up cooling units, stressing aging grids. Ironically, the extra electricity often comes from fossil-fuel plants that emit more heat-trapping gases. Mitigation must therefore address both surface materials and energy sources. Ignoring the feedback loop would treat symptoms while ________ the root cause.

Vocabulary

  • mitigation 완화
  • feedback loop 피드백 루프
  • amplify 증폭시키다
24 Question
  1. amplifying
  2. diagnosing
  3. alleviating
  4. forecasting
  5. resolving
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 25]

Thousands of satellites orbit Earth, providing navigation, weather data, and broadband signals. Each launch, however, leaves behind fragments when stages collide or explode. Even paint chips traveling at orbital speed can puncture spacecraft hulls, creating more debris in a chain reaction known as Kessler Syndrome. Astronomers worry that a densely shrouded sky will obscure telescope observations and jeopardize future missions. Proposed solutions include harpoons, drag sails, and laser nudges, but none have scaled commercially. Meanwhile, private companies rush to deploy mega-constellations, multiplying traffic ten-fold. Failure to enact binding regulations could render low-Earth orbit ________ for decades.

Vocabulary

  • shrouded 뒤덮인
  • jeopardize 위태롭게 하다
  • hazardous 위험한
25 Question
  1. navigable
  2. irrelevant
  3. pristine
  4. hazardous
  5. transparent

[지문 26]

Antibiotics revolutionized medicine, yet their success fostered complacency. Doctors once prescribed them for viral colds at patient insistence, accelerating resistance evolution. Bacteria swap genetic instructions like trading cards, spreading immunity across species. New drugs enter the market slowly because profits are slim compared with chronic-disease treatments. Public health campaigns stress completing prescriptions, but misinformation online promotes halving doses to "avoid chemicals." Laboratories race to develop phage therapies that target microbes with surgical precision. Unless stewardship improves, researchers warn of a post-antibiotic era where minor cuts become lethal. Overuse today could forfeit tomorrow’s miracle cures to microbial ________.

Vocabulary

  • forfeit 몰수당하다
  • stewardship 관리
  • dominance 우세, 지배
26 Question
  1. vulnerability
  2. dominance
  3. dormancy
  4. gratitude
  5. equilibrium

[지문 27]

Remote work promised quiet focus for introverts, but home offices teem with distractions of another kind. Doorbells ring, children demand snacks, and neighbors drill holes during video calls. Boundaries between professional and personal life blur when the dining table doubles as a desk. Paradoxically, some employees now overwork to prove they are not slacking off. Slack pings replace hallway conversations, extending the workday into late evening. Employers experiment with "focus Fridays" and asynchronous updates, yet culture shifts slowly. Without intentional routines, the flexibility that once seemed liberating can morph into time-zone-less ________.

  • teem with ~으로 가득하다
  • morph into ~으로 변하다
  • chaos 혼돈

  2. chaos
  3. ritual
  4. leisure
  5. sanctuary

[지문 28]

High-school curricula often omit financial literacy, leaving teenagers to learn about credit cards through marketing emails. Interest-free grace periods lure novices into spending beyond their means. Compound interest works against them when balances roll over, snowballing into debt that shadows early adulthood. Smartphones make transactions frictionless, masking the psychological pain that cash once imposed. Economists argue that informed consent in lending requires understanding exponential math, not just signature boxes. Absent mandatory education, the convenience of plastic can translate into years of repayment. Teaching students the true cost of borrowing would illuminate the hidden price tag on ________ consumption.

Vocabulary

  • omit 생략하다
  • frictionless 마찰이 없는
  • deferred 연기된, 미뤄진
28 Question
  1. deferred
  2. frugal
  3. ethical
  4. instantaneous
  5. communal

[지문 29]

Coffee enjoys a wellness halo thanks to studies linking moderate consumption with reduced disease risk. Headlines, however, often exaggerate correlational findings, presenting daily lattes as lifesaving medicine. Dietary researchers struggle to isolate caffeine effects from confounding lifestyle variables. Meanwhile, high-sugar flavored drinks dominate café menus, blurring the line between beverage and dessert. When health claims drive sales, scaling up portion sizes becomes profitable marketing. Public discourse rarely distinguishes black coffee from whipped-cream concoctions. By treating all forms equally, consumers risk embracing sugary excess under the banner of antioxidant virtue, a decision that ________ the original research.

Vocabulary

  • halo 후광
  • confounding 교란하는
  • misrepresent 잘못 표현하다

  2. misrepresents
  3. replicates
  4. validates
  5. codifies

[지문 30]

Deep underground in Norway’s Svalbard archipelago lies a vault storing seeds from around the globe. The facility, chilled by permafrost, acts as insurance against crop failure from climate change or war. Every envelope contains genetic diversity painstakingly collected by local farmers over centuries. Yet some critics argue that preservation without cultivation risks freezing biodiversity in a museum, disconnected from evolving pests. Others counter that rescue copies are indispensable when political turmoil destroys regional stocks. Regular withdrawals and deposits mimic a savings account: living seeds must germinate periodically to stay viable. Treating the vault as a static archive would ignore its role as an active ________ of agricultural resilience.

Vocabulary

  • archipelago 군도
  • painstakingly 공들여
  • engine 엔진, 동력

Virtual-reality clinics now help patients confront fears ranging from public speaking to flying. Therapists control every variable: turbulence intensity, audience size, even the hum of an airplane engine. Because the scenarios unfold in a headset, exposure can stop the moment distress rises too high, keeping treatment safe. Each session nudges the patient’s tolerance a little further, building confidence that spills into real-world situations. Decades of research show that facing a phobia in small, repeatable steps rewires neural threat pathways. The headset merely provides a convenient stage for the oldest principle in behavioral science. Recovery hinges on ________ exposure, not on graphic fidelity.

Some users upload raw data to third-party sites that compare genetic markers for disease risk. Privacy experts caution that once sequenced, your genome cannot be ‘‘changed like a password.’’ Insurance companies and law enforcement already explore ways to mine public databases. Family ties also complicate consent: your profile inadvertently exposes relatives who never took the test. Start-ups envision swarms of autonomous drones zipping above traffic to deliver take-out meals in under ten minutes. Early pilots succeeded in suburban cul-de-sacs, but dense downtown airspace poses harder problems. Rooftop wind shear rattles navigation sensors; pedestrians dislike constant propeller noise. City planners warn that sky lanes could become as jammed as streets if left unregulated. Historians note that automobiles were once welcomed as a cure for horse-drawn chaos—until they created their own gridlock. Unless authorities coordinate flight corridors and sharing protocols, drone deliveries may simply relocate urban ________ from asphalt to open air.

Forest rangers once relied on lookout towers and binoculars to spot illegal logging, reacting days after chainsaws roared. Today, satellites circle the equator every ninety minutes, photographing the same hillside again within hours. Algorithms compare pixel changes to flag tree loss in near real time, sending alerts to smartphones thousands of kilometers away. Rapid response teams can seize equipment before trunks even hit the ground. Conservationists celebrate the technology but remind donors that prosecution still depends on local courts. Eyes in the sky deter wrongdoing only when followed by consequences on earth. High-resolution imagery alone cannot stop ________ logging.

Instead, many workers retreat behind noise-canceling headphones to escape chatter and ringing phones. Studies using wearable sensors show that face-to-face interaction actually declines after walls come down. Constant auditory interruption forces the brain to context-switch, draining glucose with each pivot. Over time employees schedule meetings just to secure a quiet conference room for focused tasks. Companies now install “privacy pods,” reinventing cubicles they demolished a decade earlier. When repayments recycle into new loans, entire communities diversify beyond subsistence agriculture. Researchers track increases in children’s school attendance and household nutrition following credit access. Proponents claim scarce patrols are allocated more efficiently; civil-rights groups counter that past arrests reflect biased policing, not objective danger. Feedback loops arise when heightened patrol presence generates more reports, feeding the algorithm fresh justification to stay. Transparency audits reveal that seemingly neutral variables—like school truancy or broken windows—act as proxies for race and income. Reformers propose community oversight and rigorous fairness metrics before algorithms influence resource allocation. Scrolling a bright phone screen in bed feels harmless, yet blue-wavelength light tells the suprachiasmatic nucleus that noon has arrived. Melatonin release stalls, delaying the cascade of physiological changes that prepare cells for repair. Researchers liken nighttime screen time to a mini-time-zone jump executed daily, confusing internal clocks. Sleep depth suffers even when total hours appear sufficient, leaving people foggy and irritable by afternoon. Sales of amber glasses and ‘‘dark mode’’ apps soar, but behavior change lags behind convenience. True recovery requires respecting the evolutionary bargain struck with sunset. Aggregate maps reveal fungal migrations triggered by climate change, offering clues to forest health. The hobby thus evolves into a crowdsourced sensor network spanning continents at virtually no cost. Data quality improves as machine-learning filters sift blurry images before they reach taxonomists. Cognitive scientists suggest that tactile page turning anchors spatial memory, letting learners recall a formula’s approximate location on the paper. Screens encourage scrolling, which flattens the mental map into one continuous ribbon. Frequent notifications also fracture concentration, prompting re-reading that feels familiar but shallow. Massive cargo ships crisscross the world’s oceans twenty-four hours a day, their engines emitting a low rumble that carries for hundreds of kilometers underwater. Biologists once thought the din harmless, but hydrophone records now reveal a steady upward creep in background noise since the 1960s. Baleen whales use infrasonic calls to locate mates and feeding grounds, yet those signals fade beneath the mechanical hum. Some pods shorten their songs; others raise the volume, expending precious energy. Calves that stray too far can lose contact entirely, reducing survival odds. Conservationists propose slower cruising speeds and bubble-wrap propellers as partial fixes. Psychologists call these curated feeds “highlight reels,” because they omit mundane struggles that fill most lives. Repeated exposure skews perception, making ordinary routines feel inadequate by comparison. Users try to keep up, staging their own portraits for the algorithm’s approval. Epidemiologists caution that translation to human disease remains preliminary, yet public concern has already spiked. Beverage companies advertise “micro-filter” bottles while cities debate upgraded filtration plants. Satellite constellations now photograph wheat fields every three days, translating subtle shifts in greenness into biomass estimates. Agronomists overlay moisture data and soil chemistry to forecast yields months before harvest. Early warnings let cooperatives lock in futures contracts and pre-position grain storage, smoothing price swings. Extension agents text farmers tailored advice about nitrogen or irrigation tweaks, saving both money and groundwater. Yet the torrent of data can overwhelm smallholders lacking broadband or analytics skills. Partnerships with local co-ops aim to bridge the gap by providing dashboard printouts at weekly meetings. Transportation planners argue that twenty-four-hour transit would increase operating costs, yet spreadsheets seldom capture the social expense of stranded workers. Late-night service trials in several metros showed modest ridership but significant reductions in drunk-driving incidents. Emergency crews reported faster hospital transfers because roads carried fewer private cars. Opponents claim empty trains waste electricity, but renewable grids can accommodate off-peak demand. Psychologists studying “eco-anxiety” find that overwhelming imagery can paralyze action, causing viewers to tune out or despair. Community organizers therefore pair sobering statistics with attainable steps, such as neighborhood solar co-ops or tree-planting drives. Small victories build collective efficacy, reinforcing the belief that individual choices matter. Without constructive outlets, fear curdles into resignation and social withdrawal. The human brain is remarkably adept at filtering out irrelevant sensory information. In a bustling café, you can focus on a single conversation while the clatter of dishes and the murmur of other patrons fade into the background. This ability, known as the "cocktail party effect," allows us to navigate complex social environments. However, this same filtering mechanism can make us blind to gradual changes. We may not notice a slowly worsening leak in the ceiling or the subtle decline in a colleague's performance. Our perceptual system is wired to detect sudden shifts, not creeping transformations. Therefore, what serves as an advantage in one context—ignoring constant, predictable stimuli—can become a significant ________ in another.

In the digital age, information is abundant, but wisdom is scarce. The internet provides instant answers to factual questions, from the capital of Mongolia to the boiling point of water. Yet, this easy access can discourage the deeper, more effortful thinking required for true understanding. We collect facts without connecting them, mistaking a full memory for a developed mind. The challenge is not finding information but evaluating it, synthesizing disparate sources, and applying knowledge to novel problems. This process requires patience and critical thought—qualities that the fast-paced, notification-driven digital world often undermines. Consequently, while we may be more informed than any previous generation, we are not necessarily more ________.

The concept of "food miles"—the distance food travels from farm to plate—has become a popular shorthand for environmental impact. Consumers often choose local produce, assuming it has a smaller carbon footprint. However, a comprehensive lifecycle analysis reveals a more complex picture. For many foods, transportation accounts for only a small fraction of total emissions. The energy used in farming, processing, and storage is often far more significant. A tomato grown in a heated greenhouse in a cold climate, for instance, may have a larger footprint than one shipped from a sunnier region, even after accounting for transport. Therefore, focusing solely on distance can be misleading. A more effective approach to reducing the environmental cost of our food is to consider the ________ of production, not just the location.

While these activities can be enjoyable, their effects on workplace dynamics are often short-lived. The skills practiced in an artificial, high-stakes game do not always transfer to the mundane realities of office life. A person who cooperates to solve a puzzle may still hoard information or avoid difficult conversations back at their desk. True teamwork is not built in a single afternoon. It grows from consistent, daily habits: clear communication, mutual respect, and shared accountability for outcomes. The rise of automation has fueled anxieties about mass unemployment. While it is true that robots and AI are replacing certain types of manual and routine cognitive labor, history shows that technological change also creates new jobs. The invention of the automobile displaced blacksmiths and carriage makers, but it also created millions of jobs in manufacturing, road construction, and the service industry. Similarly, the computer revolution eliminated many clerical roles but spawned new professions in software development and IT support. The primary challenge is not a permanent lack of work, but rather the need for workforce ________. Education systems and job training programs must adapt to equip people with the skills required for the new roles that emerge alongside new technologies.

In the world of architecture, the phrase "form follows function" has long been a guiding principle. It suggests that the shape of a building or object should be primarily based upon its intended purpose. This idea was a reaction against the ornate and decorative styles of the 19th century, where aesthetics often took precedence over practicality. Proponents of this principle argue that a well-designed object, from a simple chair to a massive skyscraper, achieves its beauty through its efficiency and fitness for purpose. However, critics contend that a strict adherence to this rule can lead to sterile, uninspired designs that ignore the human need for beauty and delight. They argue that the best designs achieve a ________ between form and function, where neither is sacrificed for the other.

  • precedence 우선

[지문 57]

The "bystander effect" is a well-documented social psychological phenomenon in which individuals are less likely to offer help to a victim when other people are present. The greater the number of bystanders, the less likely it is that any one of them will help. This occurs due to a "diffusion of responsibility," where each person assumes that someone else will intervene. Additionally, people often look to others for cues on how to behave. If no one else is reacting to an emergency, individuals may interpret the situation as less serious than it actually is. This pluralistic ignorance can lead to tragic inaction. Understanding this effect is the first step to overcoming it; by being aware of this tendency, individuals can make a conscious choice to ________.

Vocabulary

  • bystander 방관자
  • diffusion 분산, 확산
  • pluralistic ignorance 다원적 무지

  1. follow the crowd
  2. wait for instructions
  3. assess the danger
  4. take personal responsibility

[지문 58]

The concept of a "growth mindset," popularized by psychologist Carol Dweck, posits that abilities and intelligence can be developed through dedication and hard work. This contrasts with a "fixed mindset," which assumes that these qualities are innate and unchangeable. Students with a growth mindset tend to embrace challenges, persist in the face of setbacks, and see effort as a path to mastery. They are more likely to learn from criticism and find inspiration in the success of others. Conversely, those with a fixed mindset often avoid challenges to prevent failure, give up easily, and feel threatened by the success of their peers. The implications for education are profound. By praising effort and strategy rather than just intelligence or talent, teachers and parents can help cultivate a growth mindset in children, thereby fostering a lifelong love of ________.

Vocabulary

  • posit 상정하다, 가정하다
  • innate 타고난
  • cultivate 함양하다, 기르다

  2. learning
  3. comfort
  4. validation
  5. perfection

[지문 59]

The placebo effect is a fascinating phenomenon where a patient experiences real improvements in their condition after receiving a treatment with no active therapeutic properties, such as a sugar pill. This effect is not merely "in the patient's head"; studies have shown measurable physiological changes, such as the release of endorphins, in response to a placebo. The patient's belief in the treatment and the positive expectations communicated by the doctor play a crucial role. This highlights the powerful connection between the mind and body. The placebo effect demonstrates that our ________ can have a direct and tangible impact on our physical health.

Vocabulary

  • placebo 위약, 가짜 약
  • physiological 생리적인
  • tangible 실질적인, 만질 수 있는
59 Question
  1. diet
  2. genetics
  3. social status
  4. physical environment
  5. beliefs and expectations
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 60]

In ecology, a keystone species is a species that has a disproportionately large effect on its natural environment relative to its abundance. The removal of a keystone species can cause a dramatic shift in the ecosystem, often leading to a decrease in biodiversity. For example, sea otters in the Pacific Northwest are a keystone species because they prey on sea urchins. Without the otters, the sea urchin population would explode and devour the kelp forests, destroying the habitat for many other species. Thus, the presence of a relatively small number of sea otters is crucial for maintaining the ________ of the entire kelp forest ecosystem.

Vocabulary

  • keystone species 핵심종
  • disproportionately 불균형적으로
  • stability 안정성
60 Question
  1. temperature
  2. simplicity
  3. profitability
  4. stability
  5. isolation
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 61]

Portable translation earbuds now stream simultaneous subtitles into a wearer’s ear, bridging language gaps on business trips. Early adopters rave about friction-free networking, yet linguists warn that idioms and humor often survive only in cultural context. A Japanese pun rendered literally into English can drain a joke of its sparkle, leaving awkward silence. Overreliance on machine output may therefore lull travelers into thinking they grasp subtle cues. Diplomatic schools still mandate months of immersion because live rapport grows from shared references, not mere syntax. Technology shines when vocabulary is all that separates speakers, but diplomacy depends on more than nouns and verbs. To avoid faux pas, globetrotters must remember to ________ instead of blindly trusting silicon interpreters.

Vocabulary

  • simultaneous 동시의
  • rapport 라포, 친밀한 관계
  • read the room 분위기를 파악하다

  2. mute device latency
  3. memorize word lists
  4. ignore body language
  5. replace local guides

Countries rich in sunshine and coastline plan to ship “green hydrogen” abroad by converting it to ammonia, loading it onto tankers, and cracking it back on arrival. Engineers celebrate the carbon-free energy loop, but economists highlight volatility in fertilizer demand that already dictates ammonia prices. A bumper grain season can spike consumption, while drought depresses it, complicating revenue forecasts. Energy planners therefore urge separate market mechanisms to stabilize cash flow. Insurance products might buffer producers against swings unrelated to their fuel customers. Absent hedging tools, investors could flee the sector after a single price crash. Long-term viability rests on policies that ________ rather than magnify commodity shocks.

  • bumper 풍작의

  4. cap export volumes
  5. ignore price signals

Telemedicine boomed during lockdowns as clinicians diagnosed rashes and coughs over video chat. Rural patients saved hours of travel, yet physicians noticed that subtle tremors or gait changes often go unseen through a webcam. High-resolution cameras help, but they cannot replicate hands-on palpation or measure joint resistance. Hybrid care models now combine remote check-ins with periodic in-person exams. Hospitals allocate scarce appointment slots to complex cases flagged by digital triage. The goal is not to digitize everything but to delegate routine monitoring to pixels, freeing human touch for nuanced puzzles. Success hinges on workflows that ________ instead of replace physical assessment.

Data flooded in from window boxes and rooftop hives, revealing unexpected pollinator highways. Traditional funding agencies took note and provided follow-up awards. Yet some critics argue that popularity contests favor photogenic creatures over vital microbes hidden in soil. Gecko-inspired adhesives cling to glass without glue, relying on microscopic hairs that exploit van der Waals forces. Engineers printing synthetic versions claim future skyscraper cleaners will stroll up windows like reptiles. Field tests, however, reveal rapid performance drops when dust coats the material. Nature solves this by shedding skin, presenting fresh pads after each climb. Researchers now prototype peel-away film layers to mimic the renewal process. "Zero-trust" frameworks now verify every request regardless of location, encrypting micro-chunks of data in transit. Usability studies reveal that frequent log-ins increase risky behaviors like writing passwords on sticky notes. The ultimate goal is a self-sustaining lunar greenhouse where astronauts harvest salad beside regolith. Language-learning apps bombard users with daily streak counters and cartoon trophies, funnelling them toward multiple-choice drills. Linguists argue that passive recognition differs from active recall needed for spontaneous conversation. Some platforms now integrate AI chat partners that adapt to learner errors in real time. Early studies show higher speaking confidence but also highlight over-politeness compared with real-world slang. Pedagogical value depends on dialogue engines that ________.

Vocabulary

  • bombard 퍼붓다
  • pedagogical 교육학적인
  • approximate street speech 실제 구어에 근접하다
90 Question
  1. approximate street speech
  2. freeze formal scripts
  3. mute corrective feedback
  4. award random badges
  5. obscure pronunciation hints
Vera's Flavor편입영어 독해 Basic 편
[지문 91]

Blockchain advocates tout decentralized finance as environmentally benign, yet verifying transactions on certain networks now consumes more electricity than small nations. Renewable-energy credits partially offset emissions, but critics say offsets merely shuffle carbon on paper. Energy policy analysts call for protocols that reward efficiency improvements and penalize wasteful consensus mechanisms. New “proof-of-stake” models show promise, though adoption lags entrenched interests. Transparent dashboards could display real-time wattage, pressuring miners to migrate to greener grids. Planet-wide digital ledgers should not undermine planetary health. Sustainable crypto-economies will hinge on accounting systems that ________ across environmental dimensions.

Vocabulary

  • benign 무해한
  • entrenched 견고한, 확립된
  • weigh emissions holistically 배출량을 총체적으로 평가하다
91 Question
  1. weigh emissions holistically
  2. track tokens publicly
  3. expand hash incentives
  4. ignore electricity markets
  5. celebrate record prices

[지문 92]

Start-ups offer AI voice-cloning that can mimic anyone after a minute of audio, opening doors for personalized assistants and movie dubbing. Journalists, however, warn of phone scams where relatives’ voices plead for urgent cash. Deep-fake election robocalls already sow distrust, hinting at larger disinformation campaigns. Legislators debate consent registries and watermark mandates, but enforcement across borders remains murky. Audio forensics teams race to develop spectral signatures that flag synthetic intonation. Public education about verification lags behind adoption curves. Safeguarding civil discourse will require guardrails that ________ in the era of synthetic speech.

Vocabulary

  • dubbing 더빙
  • murky 흐린, 불분명한
  • safeguard public trust 대중의 신뢰를 보호하다
92 Question
  1. safeguard public trust
  2. monetize vocal likeness
  3. silence whistle-blowers
  4. blur identity lines
  5. exaggerate novelty claims

[지문 93]

Beachfront towns rebrand as “digital-nomad havens,” offering free coworking passes to lure remote developers. Rental apps soon list studio flats at tourist prices, pushing teachers and fishers toward inland commutes. Local councils propose tiered tax rates to funnel revenue into affordable housing, but enforcement proves tricky when leases go private. Community organizers host dialogue circles where newcomers and longtime residents share neighborhood histories. Sociologists observe that cultural friction eases when both groups volunteer in the same cleanup drives. Balancing economic influx with social cohesion depends on zoning and taxation tools that ________ instead of pricing locals out.

  • haven 안식처
  • cohesion 결속
  • protect local affordability 지역의 구매력을 보호하다
93 Question
  1. protect local affordability
  2. mirror global demand
  3. amplify short-stay profits
  4. mandate digital visas
  5. glamorize remote luxury

[지문 94]

Agricultural feedlots boost growth by adding low-dose antibiotics to grain, a practice veterinarians decry as a breeding ground for superbugs. Residues seep into waterways, where resistant genes hop between species. Some supermarket chains now source “antibiotic-free” meat, but labeling inconsistencies confuse shoppers. Pilot programs vaccinate livestock against common infections, reducing prophylactic pill counts. Policymakers debate import tariffs on drug-dependent beef to level competitive costs. A post-antibiotic menu will emerge only through standards that ________ for both barn and biosphere.

Vocabulary

  • feedlot 가축 사육장
  • decry 비난하다
  • reserve critical antibiotics 필수 항생제를 보존하다
94 Question
  1. reserve critical antibiotics
  2. halve daily rations
  3. intensify feedlot density
  4. subsidize broad-spectrum use
  5. obscure residue tests

[지문 95]

Carebots in elder-care facilities dispense medication reminders, but residents still crave human laughter over synthetic chirps. Trials show improved adherence yet stagnant loneliness scores. Designers add plush exteriors and customizable voices, hoping to trigger emotional warmth. Gerontologists caution that genuine companionship arises from unpredictable stories and tactile nuance. Staffing ratios rarely improve when robots arrive, feeding fears of high-tech isolation. Success will hinge on deployment models that ________ rather than sideline caregivers.

  • adherence 준수
  • gerontologist 노인학자

  2. replace night shifts
  3. gamify pill intake
  4. harvest patient data
  5. dictate social schedules

[지문 96]

Restoring drained urban peatlands could lock away gigatons of CO₂ and buffer neighborhoods against flash floods. Yet real-estate developers eye the reclaimed land for high-rise projects promising tax revenue. Ecologists map methane fluxes to show that water-logged soils must remain saturated to stay net-negative in emissions. Community workshops explore boardwalk parks that offer recreation without excavation. Funding proposals bundle carbon credits with green-space grants, aligning environmental and civic goals. Long-term climate strategy depends on land-use rules that ________ for generations.

Vocabulary

  • peatland 이탄지
  • buffer 완충하다
  • store carbon naturally 자연적으로 탄소를 저장하다

  2. drain wetlands annually
  3. expedite concrete pours
  4. privatize public marshes
  5. normalize peat fires

Venture capital pours in on rumored milestones, inflating valuations beyond near-term capacity. Engineers test spacecraft lasers that melt aluminum shards in orbit, turning debris into propellant for on-board thrusters. The circular-fuel concept could defray cleanup costs by powering itself with trash. Material analyses must verify plume toxicity to sensitive telescope lenses nearby. Insurance underwriters await collision-risk models before backing salvage fleets. Diplomatic channels negotiate salvage rights for fragments originating from multiple nations. Viable space sanitation will rest on treaties that ________ instead of stoke sovereignty disputes.

[지문 100]

Wave-energy converters promise round-the-clock power, unlike sunlight-dependent panels, yet saltwater corrosion chews through metal joints. Engineers trial composite polymers and self-healing coatings inspired by squid beaks. Maintenance crews schedule dives during calm tides, but storms can demolish moorings overnight. Predictive sensors relay strain data to shore, triggering preemptive shutdowns. Lenders demand decade-long durability studies before financing coastal arrays. Commercial viability depends on devices that ________ under relentless surf.

Vocabulary

  • corrosion 부식
  • preemptive 선제적인
  • withstand marine forces 해양의 힘을 견디다
100 Question
  1. withstand marine forces
  2. fold after hurricanes
  3. resell excess rust
  4. inflate insurance rates
  5. prefer freshwater sites
